valid.name: add-check-without-fk
valid.code: ALTER TABLE orders ADD CONSTRAINT positive_amount CHECK (amount > 0);

valid.name: add-fk-not-valid
valid.code:
ALTER TABLE orders
  ADD CONSTRAINT orders_customer_fk FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers (id) NOT VALID;

valid.name: never-validating
valid.code: ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent (id);

valid.name: validate
valid.code: ALTER TABLE orders VALIDATE CONSTRAINT orders_customer_fk;

invalid.name: add-fk
invalid.code:
ALTER TABLE orders
  ADD CONSTRAINT orders_customer_fk FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers (id);

errors.message: Adding a foreign key without `NOT VALID` validates every existing row under an `ACCESS EXCLUSIVE` lock that blocks writers. Use `ADD CONSTRAINT ... FOREIGN KEY (...) REFERENCES ... NOT VALID`, then `ALTER TABLE ... VALIDATE CONSTRAINT` in a separate migration (`VALIDATE` only takes a `SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE` lock).

invalid.name: never-not-valid
invalid.code: ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent (id) NOT VALID;

errors.message: Avoid `NOT VALID` on foreign keys. The constraint is added in a non-validated state and won't reject existing violations until you remember to run `VALIDATE CONSTRAINT`; some projects prefer to fail loudly at constraint-add time instead.
